result:
I've written `root-cause.md`. Here's the summary:

## Root cause
**`reference/slugify/slugify.py:56`** in `smart_truncate`, the no-separator branch:
```python
cutoff = max_length if len(string) > max_length else len(string) - 1
```
By line 56 the function has already guaranteed `len(string) >= max_length` (the early return at line 49). So the `else` only fires when `len(string) == max_length`, where it computes `max_length - 1` and chops the last character off a word that actually fits exactly. The `- 1` is a misplaced off-by-one.

## The trigger is narrow
The bug fires only when **`word_boundary=True`, the slug has no separator (single word), and its length is exactly `max_length`** — matching the editor's "titles right at the limit" hunch. The three "working" cases all dodge it: longer-than-cap takes the `len > max_length → cutoff = max_length` branch; word-boundary-off returns `string[:max_length]` at line 53; multi-word slugs go through the `split(separator)` loop instead.

## Minimal fix
Drop the `- 1` (line 56): `cutoff = max_length`. Verified: the failing case becomes `'newsletter'` (10), and the three already-correct cases are unchanged.

## Why tests miss it
Two gaps coincide. Every `word_boundary=True` test uses **multi-word, dash-containing** input, so it flows through the `split` loop and never touches line 56. The one no-separator test (`test_smart_truncate_no_seperator`, 24 chars vs `max_length=100`) **early-returns at line 49** before reaching the bug. Nothing exercises the exact conjunction (no separator + length ≥ cap + word boundary) that reaches the defect.

I confirmed all of this by running the library directly — no source was modified.
